sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 82, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Initial had 3 c boxes + full center bar. Current has 2 c + 1 W + shrunken center bar (separated from gray interior). Boxes moved from initial positions. Center bar shrank as boxes were activated.
- Plan: Try moving player LEFT toward center bar to see if bar expands or changes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 81 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving LEFT twice:
- Player N at [28-30, 28-31] (moved left)
- Center bar b at [28-31, 32-39] (moved right!)
- Gray interior g at [29-30, 32-38] (moved right with center bar)
- W box at [31-35, 28-31] (moved left to follow player)
- c box at [28-31, 16-19] (unchanged)
- c box at [32-35, 20-23] (unchanged)
So the center bar and gray interior moved when the player moved! The center bar is now at [28-31, 32-39], which is to the RIGHT of the player. The W box moved to [31-35, 28-31], which is below the player.
This is a key insight! The center bar and gray interior move relative to the player. When the player moves LEFT, the center bar moves RIGHT. When the player moves RIGHT, the center bar moves LEFT.
Let me think about this. The center bar seems to be "opposite" the player. When the player is on the right side, the center bar is on the left. When the player is on the left side, the center bar is on the right.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Before: Player at [28-30, 36-39], center bar at [28-31, 28-35]
- After: Player at [28-30, 28-31], center bar at [28-31, 32-39]
The player moved from col 36-39 to col 28-31 (left by ~8 cols).
The center bar moved from col 28-35 to col 32-39 (right by ~4 cols).
Wait, that's not symmetric. Let me think again.
Actually, the center bar might be "attached" to the W box. When the W box moves, the center bar moves too. Or the center bar might be "attached" to the player.
Let me look at the positions:
- Before: Player at [28-30, 36-39], W box at [31-35, 36-39], center bar at [28-31, 28-35]
- After: Player at [28-30, 28-31], W box at [31-35, 28-31], center bar at [28-31, 32-39]
The player and W box both moved left. The center bar moved right.
Hmm, the center bar seems to move in the opposite direction of the player. Or maybe the center bar is "pushed" by the player.
Let me think about what the goal might be. The initial state had the center bar at [28-31, 28-39] with the gray interior inside. The current state has the center bar at [28-31, 32-39] with the gray interior separate.
Wait, the center bar is now at [28-31, 32-39], which is 8 cols wide (same as before). The gray interior is at [29-30, 32-38], which is 7 cols wide (same as before).
So the center bar and gray interior moved but didn't change size. The center bar is still 8 cols wide, and the gray interior is still 7 cols wide.
The initial center bar was 12 cols wide, and the initial gray interior was 10 cols wide. So the center bar and gray interior are still shrunken.
Let me think about what causes the center bar to shrink. It shrank when I activated boxes. Maybe the center bar shrinks when boxes are activated, and it expands when boxes are deactivated.
But I've been activating and deactivating boxes, and the center bar hasn't expanded. Let me try deactivating the W box by pressing SPACE.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game mechanics. The W box follows the player. When the player moves, the W box moves to follow. The center bar moves in the opposite direction.
Maybe the goal is to have the center bar in a specific position, or to have the center bar and gray interior in a specific configuration.
Let me try moving the player to the right side and see if the center bar moves to the left side, potentially overlapping with the initial position.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The initial state had:
- Player at [49-51, 32-35] (bottom)
- Center bar at [28-31, 28-39] (center)
The current state has:
- Player at [28-30, 28-31] (center-left)
- Center bar at [28-31, 32-39] (center-right)
The player and center bar are at the same row range [28-31]. In the initial state, the player was at [49-51], which is much lower.
Maybe the goal is to move the player back to the initial position [49-51, 32-35]. Let me try moving DOWN.
But wait, the W box is at [31-35, 28-31], which is below the player. If I move DOWN, the player might collide with the W box.
Let me try moving DOWN and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player and W box move together. Center bar moves opposite to player. Center bar still shrunken (8 cols vs initial 12). Gray interior still separate.
Plan: Try moving player DOWN to see if center bar moves and potentially restores to initial state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving DOWN
result = action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After DOWN x5:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'G', 'W', 'N', 'b', 'g'] and n['pixels'] >= 8:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], px={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")
print(f"Level completed: {result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  color=g, rows [29-30], cols [29-38], px=20, children=[]
  color=c, rows [32-35], cols [20-23], px=12, children=[6]
  color=N, rows [48-50], cols [28-31], px=12, children=[]
  color=W, rows [51-55], cols [28-31], px=16, children=[9]
  color=c, rows [63-63], cols [36-63], px=28, children=[]
